---
title: Computational Methods to Reduce Bias in Binary Endpoints in Dose-finding Studies
title_short: Computational Methods to Reduce Bias in Binary Endpoints in Dose-finding Studies
subtitle: "CEN Warschau 2026"
institute: "Institute of Medical Biometry \\break University of Heidelberg"
author: "Alexandra Balzer"
author_short: "Alexandra Balzer"
date: "2026-05-21"
date-format: "DD.MM.YYYY"
format: 
  beamer:
    template: markdown_beamer_template.tex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
linestretch: 1.1
header-includes:
    - \input{imbi_layout_markdownbeamer_en.tex}
    - \usepackage{tikz}
    - \usetikzlibrary{decorations.pathreplacing}
bibliography: bibliography.bib
biblio-title: "References"
classoption: "aspectratio=169"
# Uncomment the following to two lines and insert the output path
# knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) {
#  rmarkdown::render(inputFile, encoding = "UTF-8", output_dir = "Put/output/path/here",  output_file = paste0("STUDYTITLE_report_", Sys.Date()) )})
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    echo = FALSE
)
```

```{r dependencies, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(gridExtra)
library(kableExtra)
library(gt)
```

# Introduction 

### Introduction & Previous Work

\pause


* Optimization of dose finding and selection is a key goal in oncology drug research.
* OPTIMUS Project: FDA initiative to improve dose optimization/dose finding.
* Key challenge: Accurate estimation of therapeutic effects
  * True treatment effect is often overestimated in Phase II
  * Phase III results may not confirm Phase II findings.
* Bias-correction methods were presented for normally distributed outcomes (e.g. boostrap methods) ([@tianyu2024])
* No existing methods for binary endpoints identified in a literature research.


### Setup

* $I$ active treatment arms with $n$ patients.
* Response $X_{ij}$ for patient $j$ in group $i$ with $X_{ij} \sim \textrm{Ber}(\theta_i)$ and $i = 1, \dots, I$. 
* $\mathbf{X_i} = (X_{i1}, \dots, X_{in})$ vector of responses for group $i$ and $\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{X_1}, \dots, \mathbf{X_I}).$


* Parameter of interest is $\theta_{max} = \max(\theta_1, \dots, \theta_I).$
* Estimator:  $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}) = \max[\bar{\mathbf{X}}_1, \dots ,\bar{\mathbf{X}}_I]$.
* Even if $\bar{\mathbf{X}}_i = \mathrm{mean}(X_{i1}, \dots, X_{in})$ is an unbiased estimator, $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$ is biased.


<!-- ### Idea of the Bootstrap Method -->

<!-- * The bias of the initial estimator is:  -->
<!-- $$\mathrm{Bias} = \mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})] - \theta_{max}.$$ -->
<!-- * Bootstrap **B** can be used to estimate this bias:  -->
<!-- $$\widehat{\mathrm{Bias}} = \hat{\mathrm{E}}[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X_B})] - \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}).$$ -->
<!-- * This yields a bias-reduced estimator: -->
<!-- $$\hat{\theta}^{(1)}(\mathbf{X}) = \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}) - \widehat{\mathrm{Bias}}.$$ -->

### Idea of the Bootstrap Method

\pause

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.0]

% --- Row 1: Original Bias ---
\visible<1->{\draw[-] (0,4.2) -- (8,4.2);
\draw[|-|] (1,4.2) -- (5,4.2);

\draw [decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=10pt}] 
    (1, 4.3) -- (5, 4.3) 
    node[midway, above=8pt] {\footnotesize $\textrm{Bias} = \mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}(X)] - \theta_{max}$};
\node[below] at (1, 4.2) {$\theta_{max}$};
\node[below] at (5, 4.2) {$\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$};

}
% --- Row 2: Bootstrap Bias Estimate ---

\visible<2->{\draw[-] (0,2.5) -- (8,2.5);
\draw[|-|] (1,2.5) -- (5,2.5);
\draw[|-|] (5,2.5) -- (7,2.5);
\node[below] at (1, 2.5) {\small $\theta_{max}$};
\node[below] at (5, 2.5) {\small $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$};
\node[below] at (7, 2.5) {\small $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X_B})$};

\draw [decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=10pt}] 
    (5, 2.6) -- (7, 2.6) 
    node[midway, above=8pt] {\footnotesize $\widehat{\textrm{Bias}} = \mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X_B})] - \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$};
}

\visible<3->{
% --- Row 3: Bias-corrected Estimator ---
\draw[-] (0,0.4) -- (8,0.4);
\draw[|-|] (1,0.4) -- (3,0.4);
\draw[|-|] (3,0.4) -- (5,0.4);
\node[above] at (3, 0.6) {\small $\hat{\theta}^{(1)}(\mathbf{X}) = \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X}) - \widehat{\textrm{Bias}} $};
\node[below] at (1, 0.4) {\small $\theta_{max}$};
\node[below] at (5, 0.4) {\small $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{X})$};

}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

# Simulation Study

### Part I - Optimization of Bootstrap Repetitions
**Aim**: Optimize the number of bootstrap repetitions $B$ for the single and double (non-)parametric bootstrap in binary endpoints, based on bias and MSE.

* The number of bootstrap repetitions $B$ is set to 80, 100, 200, 500, and 1000.

### Part II - Bayesian Hierarchical Models

**Aim**: Identify the optimal hyperprior (with corresponding parameters) for a Bayesian hierarchical model to minimize bias and MSE.

The model is specified as: $Y_i \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n, \theta_i), \quad i = 1, \dots, I$ with

$$ \mathrm{logit}(\theta_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2), \quad \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(-1.38, 100).$$

* Hyperpriors determine how much information is pooled across groups (i.e., $\sigma^2$ controls degree of shrinkage).

* The following MCMC settings are applied fo all fits: number of chains 3, total iterations per chain: 10.000, burn-in: 2.000 .

### Part II - Priors

The priors and corresponding parameters to evaluate are based on [@Cunanan]:

* $\mathrm{Unif}[0, a]$ for $\log(\sigma), \sigma, \sigma^2$ with
  * $a \in \{1, \dots,  1000\}$.

* Half-t for $\sigma$ with
  * scale parameter $s \in \{0.5, \dots,  1000\}$ and
  * degrees of freedom $df \in \{1, \dots,  500\}$.

* Gamma distribution $\Gamma(a,b)$ for $\tau = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$ with
  * $a \in \{0.005, \dots,  2 \}$, and
  * $b \in  \{0.0005, \dots, 50\}$.
  


### General Setup
::: columns
::: column

The simulation study consists of two parts (10.000 repetitions each), with the following settings:

* 9 dose response relationships; 3 or **5** doses [@Ivanova2012]
* Maximal response: 60% or **80%**
* Sample size: n = 20 or **40** patients per dose level

:::

::: column

```{r 1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/Dose-Response.jpg")

```
:::
:::

# Results

### Part I

\pause

```{r 2, echo = F, out.width = "54%", fig.align = 'left'}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_n40_r0_gr5.png")

```

### Part I


```{r 2.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n40_r0_gr5.png")

```

### Part I: Summary

* No relevant differences in bias or MSE across bootstrap iterations.
* $B = 80$ bootstrap iterations provide adequate bias correction.
* Other simulation scenarios reached the same conclusion.

### Part II: Uniform [0, a]

\pause

```{r 4, echo = F, out.width = "56%", fig.align='left'}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/uniform_bias_cen.png")

```

### Part II:  Uniform [0, a]

```{r 4.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/uniform_cen.png")

```


### Part II: Half-t(df, s)

\pause

```{r 5, echo = F, fig.align = 'left', out.width = "55%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/half-t_bias_cen.png")

```

### Part II: Half-t(df, s)



```{r 5.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/halft_cen.png")

```

### Part II: Gamma(a,b)

```{r 7, echo = F, out.width = "55%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/gamma_bias_cen.png")

```

### Part II: Gamma(a,b)

```{r 7.1, echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Simulation final/Plots/gamma_cen.png")

```

### Part II: Summary

\begin{tabular}{l r r l r r }
\textbf{Method} & \multicolumn{4}{l}{\textbf{Parameter}} & \textbf{Bias (MSE)} \\ \hline
Uniform $\log(\sigma)$ & $a = $ & 2& &  & -0.0070 (0.00351)  \\
Uniform $\sigma$ & $a = $ & 100 & &  & -0.0047 (0.00371)  \\
Uniform $\sigma^2$ & $a = $ & 1000 & &  & 0.0003 (0.00357)  \\
Half-t & $df = $ & 100& s = & 500 & -0.0043 (0.00369)  \\
Gamma & $a = $ & 0.25& b = & 5 & 0.0004 (0.00335) 
\end{tabular}


* Uniform prior on $\sigma^2$ with $a = 1000$ and Gamma prior with $a = 0.25$ and $b = 5$ show the smallest bias.
* MSE is very similar for most parameter values and hyperpriors, except of extreme outliers.
* Prior choice depends on the true underlying dose-response scenario. 

# Conclusion

### Conclusion

* $B = 80$ bootstrap iterations are sufficient.
* Bayesian methods show promising results in reducing bias.
* Results depend on the true dose-response relationship. 
* Further scenarios for Bayesian methods and further methods will be investigated.

## Appendix


### n = 40, max response 60%, 5 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n40_r1_gr5.png")

```

### n = 40, max response 80%, 3 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n40_r0_gr3.png")

```

### n = 40, max response 60%, 3 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n40_r1_gr3.png")

```

### n = 20, max response 80%, 5 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n20_r0_gr5.png")

```

### n = 20, max response 60%, 5 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n20_r1_gr5.png")

```

### n = 20, max response 80%, 3 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n20_r0_gr3.png")

```

### n = 20, max response 60%, 3 groups


```{r , echo = F, out.width = "100%"}

knitr::include_graphics("U:/Thesis/programs/Part_I/plots/bias_mse_n20_r1_gr3.png")

```
